questions.q_athlete: You've just been handed a detailed new training programme. What's your honest first move?
questions.q_coach: You hand them a detailed new programme. What do they tend to do?

options.athlete: I study every movement and cue — I want to understand the mechanics before I start.
options.coach: Study every movement and cue — they want the mechanics before they start.

options.athlete: I push back until I understand the reasoning behind the exercise choices.
options.coach: Push back until they understand the reasoning behind your choices.

options.athlete: I just want to know how this makes me better on the competition floor — skip the filler.
options.coach: Skim past the detail — they only care how it makes them better on the floor.

options.athlete: I read it once, trust the coach, and get straight to work.
options.coach: Read it once, trust you, and get straight to work.

questions.q_athlete: You make a visible mistake at a crucial moment in training. What actually happens in your head?
questions.q_coach: They make a visible mistake at a crucial moment. How do they respond?

options.athlete: I spiral — I over-analyse what went wrong and struggle to move on.
options.coach: They spiral — over-analysing the error and struggling to move on.

options.athlete: I hate looking bad in front of people, so I quietly avoid that movement for a while.
options.coach: They hate looking bad, so they quietly avoid that movement for a while.

options.athlete: I use it as fuel. I've been underestimated before — I just grind harder.
options.coach: They use it as fuel and grind harder — they're used to proving people wrong.

options.athlete: I take full blame, even if it wasn't all mine, because I feel responsible for everyone.
options.coach: They take full blame, even when it isn't all theirs, out of responsibility for the group.

questions.q_athlete: How do you really handle a niggle, soreness, or minor tweak mid-cycle?
questions.q_coach: How do they handle a niggle, soreness, or minor tweak mid-cycle?

options.athlete: I'm hyper-aware of it — I'll keep asking whether it's normal or something serious.
options.coach: Hyper-aware — they keep asking whether it's normal or something serious.

options.athlete: I ignore it completely. Showing weakness isn't an option.
options.coach: They ignore it completely and push through, no matter the cost.

options.athlete: If I'm honest, I've used the odd ache to get out of work I didn't fancy.
options.coach: They use the odd ache to slip out of work they didn't fancy.

options.athlete: I panic a bit — I don't have the experience yet to know what's serious.
options.coach: They get anxious — they don't have the experience to know what's serious.

questions.q_athlete: A new, no-nonsense coach starts barking orders with zero explanation. What's your instinct?
questions.q_coach: A new, no-nonsense coach gives them orders with no explanation. What happens?

options.athlete: Instant distrust. You earn my buy-in before I give it.
options.coach: Instant distrust — you have to earn their buy-in before they give it.

options.athlete: I rebel internally. I want options, not orders.
options.coach: They rebel internally — they want options, not orders.

options.athlete: I work out what they want to hear so I can stay under the radar.
options.coach: They work out what the coach wants to hear and stay under the radar.

options.athlete: If it matches what a top athlete or coach online does, I'm in. Otherwise I doubt it.
options.coach: They'll follow if it matches what a top athlete or online coach does, otherwise they doubt it.

questions.q_athlete: What's your natural role in the room right before a big session or competition?
questions.q_coach: What's their natural role in the room before a big session or competition?

options.athlete: I'm the loudest — I make sure the energy's up and everyone notices.
options.coach: The loudest — making sure the energy's up and everyone notices them.

options.athlete: I'm quietly checking in on people — I'm the glue that holds us together.
options.coach: Quietly checking in on teammates — the emotional glue.

options.athlete: I sit on my own and get focused. I don't need anyone else to perform.
options.coach: On their own, fiercely self-contained — they don't need anyone to perform.

options.athlete: I keep it light and fun — I play best when I'm enjoying it, not when it's heavy.
options.coach: Keeping it light and fun — they perform best when they're enjoying it.

questions.q_athlete: Which statement is closest to the truth about your physical talent?
questions.q_coach: Which best describes their relationship with their own physical talent?

options.athlete: I don't have natural gifts — I just out-work everyone.
options.coach: Limited natural gifts, but they out-work everyone.

options.athlete: I've got the tools, but I struggle to fully believe in myself when it counts.
options.coach: Real physical tools, but their self-belief comes and goes when it counts.

options.athlete: My genetics have carried me — I've never really needed structured prep.
options.coach: Heavily reliant on genetics — they doubt they need structured prep.

options.athlete: I've got real tools and I hold myself to the highest standard to set the example.
options.coach: Strong tools and they hold themselves to the highest standard for the group.

questions.q_athlete: When training gets repetitive or monotonous, what's most likely to happen?
questions.q_coach: When training gets repetitive or monotonous, what do they do?

options.athlete: I keep going. Routine is a feature, not a bug.
options.coach: Keep going — routine is a feature, not a bug, for them.

options.athlete: I start looking for ways around the plan — a swap here, a shortcut there.
options.coach: Start finding ways around the plan — swaps, shortcuts, their own terms.

options.athlete: I lose motivation fast. Variety and creativity are what keep me locked in.
options.coach: Lose motivation fast — variety and creativity are what keep them engaged.

options.athlete: I start looking at what other coaches or athletes are doing instead.
options.coach: Start eyeing what other coaches or athletes are doing instead.

questions.q_athlete: How would the people who train alongside you describe you?
questions.q_coach: How would their training partners describe them?

options.athlete: Intense and technical — always analysing, sometimes too much.
options.coach: Intense and technical — always analysing, sometimes too much.

options.athlete: The loudest one in the room — not always for the right reasons.
options.coach: The loudest in the room — not always for the right reasons.

options.athlete: Quietly reliable, but does things their own way — don't get in the way.
options.coach: Quietly reliable but does things their own way — don't get in the way.

options.athlete: Infectious energy — makes training fun and pulls people up.
options.coach: Infectious energy — makes training fun and lifts the room.

questions.q_athlete: A coach challenges your approach and tells you to change something. Your honest response?
questions.q_coach: You challenge their approach and tell them to change something. Their honest reaction?

options.athlete: I need to know exactly why they're right before I change anything.
options.coach: They need to know exactly why you're right before they'll change.

options.athlete: I feel defensive and embarrassed, especially if people are watching.
options.coach: They get defensive and embarrassed, especially with people watching.

options.athlete: I'll say I'll change it, then mostly do what I was going to do anyway.
options.coach: They agree to your face, then mostly carry on as before.

options.athlete: I take it hard — I internalise it and replay it for hours.
options.coach: They take it hard — internalising it and replaying it for hours.

questions.q_athlete: What do you most need from a coach to do your best work?
questions.q_coach: What does this athlete most need from you to do their best work?

options.athlete: Context and reasoning — I want the thinking behind the decisions.
options.coach: Context and reasoning — the thinking behind the decisions.

options.athlete: Autonomy and variety — fewer rigid rules.
options.coach: Autonomy and variety — fewer rigid rules.

options.athlete: Patience — I'm still figuring this out.
options.coach: Patience — they're still finding their feet.

options.athlete: Consistency — earn my trust before expecting me to give it.
options.coach: Consistency — they need you to earn trust before they give it.
